retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Factors like the position of teeth in the arch also affect the prognosis of teeth having periodontitis, like the risk of mandibular canine is least while the maxillary second molar is the highest. [38] Prognosis is poor for teeth with increased periodontal probing pocket depth, mobility, bone loss, furcation involvement, malpositioned teeth, and an inadequate crown-root ratio. [39] With the improving effectiveness of oral hygiene measure practiced by the patient and discontinuation of smoking, the prognosis of the case improves along with decreasing mobility of the teeth. [39] Teeth with bleeding sites during the maintenance phase have a risk of attachment loss three times more as compared to non-bleeding sites. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Absolute dental contraindications include a hopeless dentition that is better treated with tooth removal and complete denture therapy. A hopeless dentition usually features more than two-thirds bone loss at most teeth, including canines and first molars, or significant generalized tooth mobility.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the mandible, central and lateral incisors have single roots, with central incisors often lacking apical curvature. [6] If present in either tooth, apical curvature tends to be distally inclined. [6] Mandibular canines usually present with single roots, but one unique difference between maxillary and mandibular canines is that mandibular canines may have bifurcated roots similar to those seen in maxillary first premolars. [6] The mandibular first and second premolars typically present with single roots but may have bifurcated roots in rare situations. The root tips are often inclined distally, and the mandibular second premolar's root is usually longer than the first premolar's. [7] The mandibular first and second molars usually have two distally tilted roots: a mesial and a distal root. The mandibular second molar's roots are usually shorter with less splay than the first molar roots.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary and Mandibular Dental Arch
retrieved_docs.content: Adult dentition consists of 2 central incisors, 2 lateral incisors, 2 canines, 2 first premolars, 2-second premolars, 2 first molars, 2-second molars, and 2 third molars, both in the maxilla and mandible. The teeth are secured within the alveolar ridges of the maxilla and mandible. Dentition, whether native or restored, is integral to masticatory function. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Canines – The primary teeth canines are also morphologically similar to permanent teeth canines.  The canines (tooth C, H) have a trapezoidal crown with one labial cusp. The labial surface is convex, and the palatal surface is concave. The canines have a single long root and a single root canal.  The canine roots are the longest root of all teeth. Furthermore, the upper canines have longer roots when compared to the mandibular canines. The canines serve to support the incisors. The canines have a characteristic pointed incisal edge and a large cone-shaped crown.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Research indicates that certain factors, such as being female, having retained lower primary canines, delaying treatment, and having maxillary peg lateral incisors, can increase the severity of impaction. [21] These factors should be considered when planning preventive or interceptive procedures in young patients. If left untreated, unerupted or impacted teeth can lead to numerous complications, such as pain, infection, crown resorption, root resorption and displacement of neighboring teeth, ankylosis, and infraocclusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: As compared to chronic periodontitis, aggressive periodontitis has a marked group of characteristics that defines the disease very well making it simple to diagnose it. Baer et al. 1971, defined aggressive periodontitis as "a disease of the periodontium occurring in otherwise healthy adolescent, which is characterized by a rapid loss of alveolar bone around more than one tooth of the permanent dentition." [31] The presence of local factors like calculus is not commensurate with the amount of periodontal tissue destruction. [31] The classical case presentation of aggressive periodontitis includes early age onset of disease, vertical bone loss in molars and incisor teeth leading to mobility, familial aggregation, rapid progression of the disease. [14] Aggressive periodontitis subclassifies into localized aggressive periodontitis (LAP) and generalized aggressive periodontitis (GAP). LAP is characterized by bone loss including permanent molars and incisors while GAP involved most of the permanent teeth. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function -- Intraosseous eruption
retrieved_docs.content: The initial phases of tooth eruption commence when the crown fully develops and root formation begins. [4] In this phase, an eruption pathway is created through bone resorption that facilitates the tooth's emergence. [4] Studies on dog premolars have revealed that the role of the dental follicle in tooth eruption is pivotal, as it ensures timely eruption and exfoliation of the tooth, irrespective of the crown's influence. Although earlier it was proposed that pressure from the developing tooth triggers osteoclastic activity and forms the eruption pathway, [4] this study disproves that theory. The study is outlined as follows: Alveolar resorption occlusal to the tooth persisted despite immobilizing the tooth bud by ligating the dog's mandible. [5] The unerupted tooth's crown was extracted and substituted with a metallic replica, which was subsequently inserted into the dental follicle, resulting in the normal eruption of the tooth. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Root morphology tends to follow a pattern in most individuals. In the maxilla, central and lateral incisors traditionally have single, straight, and typically conical roots. [2] Maxillary canines are also single-rooted and are the longest of all tooth roots. The maxillary first premolar often has a long root trunk with bifurcated buccal and palatal roots, but single root variants exist. [3] Maxillary premolar roots are typically thin. [3] Because of the length of the root trunk, which often spans half the length of the root, maxillary first premolars are commonly associated with inadvertent root tip fractures during extraction. Unlike the maxillary first premolar, the maxillary second premolar is usually single-rooted and rarely presents with two roots or a bifurcated root phenotypically.

retrieved_docs.title: Excessive Gingival Display -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Vertical maxillary excess is the overgrowth of the maxillary bone in the vertical plane that gives an elongated appearance to the lower half of the face. [1] It is 1 of the most common causes of excessive gingival display, and a cephalometric analysis is usually needed to confirm its diagnosis. [1] [3] Excessive gingival display results from an occlusal plane that is lower than expected. The lower lip covering the incisal edges of the upper canines and premolars is almost a pathognomonic feature of the disorder. In VME, the anterior occlusal plane continues without interruption with the posterior occlusal plane, which differs from the overeruption of incisors. [1] Even though the upper lip appears clinically short, its length is usually normal. [1] Altered Passive Eruption

retrieved_docs.title: Necrotizing Periodontal Diseases -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of NPDs is incompletely understood. Current evidence suggests that preexisting periodontal conditions may undergo rapid and destructive exacerbation under permissive host or environmental conditions. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” serves as a causal indicator and is associated with specific injury outcomes, including loss of teeth resulting from an accident (“verlies; tanden door ongeval”) as well as severe trauma such as crushing injuries or internal injuries. Recognizing this wording helps clinicians link the underlying cause to these particular clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Microbiology is identified as an applicable qualifier for the oral conditions قيلة مثانية, قيلة مستقيمية, and اضطراب اللسان, indicating that microbiological factors are relevant in the assessment or management of these disorders. Bacterial vaccines are, in turn, an applicable qualifier for microbiology, reflecting the role of immunization strategies within the broader microbiological context. The term “Without” is noted as not being associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, suggesting that the absence of a particular factor does not have a direct relationship with these specific conditions. Together, these connections highlight the importance of microbiological considerations and vaccine‑related interventions in certain oral pathologies, while clarifying that “Without” does not contribute to their etiology or presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: adverse‑event reporting and clinical management. Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2083
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to verlies; tanden door ongeval, indicating that unintended contact can result in tooth loss caused by an accident. The phrase “Due to” is also associated with verlies; tanden door ongeval, reinforcing the causal link between accidental physical contact and dental loss in traumatic situations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are both classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that each is a type of medication formulated for use within the oral cavity to manage dental or mucosal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.
